mappings.rationaleAI: Prehistoric cave art at Lascaux, Chauvet, and Altamira with animal imagery and theories about sympathetic hunting magic and shamanism directly addresses hunting rites and animal cults (A.2.d), shamanism and magic (A.2.f), and religions of prehistoric cultural stages (A.3).

The Druids as priestly elite of Celtic societies with possible human sacrifice and shamanic/divinatory roles directly address shamanism and divination (B.4.e), sacrifices (A.2.c), and the institutional role of prehistoric religious leaders (A.2.f).

Norse mythology's cosmology, creation myths, and relationship between mythology and Viking society address primitive cosmogonies (B.2.d) and the nature and function of myth in pre-Christian Scandinavian religion (B.3).

Fairy beliefs in Celtic mythology, theories about nature spirits and folk memory of displaced peoples, and the persistence of these beliefs address animism (B.2.c), the function of myth and symbol (B.3), and worship centred on natural phenomena (B.4.b).
The concept of the Apocalypse including Zoroastrian eschatological influence and millenarian movements addresses primitive and early eschatologies concerning man's destiny and cosmic renewal (B.2.d).

Dragon mythology across world cultures including theories about archetypal fears and the differences between Eastern and Western traditions addresses the function of myth and symbol (B.3) and worship centred on natural phenomena (B.4.b).

Greek mythology from oral tradition through literary codification covers creation myths, the social functions of myth, and the relationship between myth and ritual — directly relevant to the nature and function of myth (B.3).
Sacred space in religious architecture and ritual addresses the distinction between sacred and profane (B.2.a) and sacred space and places (B.2.d).
Shinto as the indigenous religious tradition of Japan with its concept of kami (spirits/gods), nature worship, and mythological texts directly addresses aboriginal religions of Asian peoples (B.5.d).
The Muses as divine inspirers of arts and the concept of divine inspiration relate to the function of myth (B.3) and worship of heroes and divine figures (B.4.d).
